posts.caption:
Before The L Word, there was KUTT: the short-lived sister zine to iconoclastic gay magazine BUTT. Lasting for just three issues between 2002 and 2003, the publication took BUTT’s signature pink pages and tongue-in-cheek attitude and applied them to a survey of the lesbian scene in Amsterdam and beyond.⁠
⁠
Over two decades on from its original run, copies of the magazine are scarce and in increasingly high demand. Responding to this reignited enthusiasm for the project, the contents of each zine have now been collated in a new book published by @idea.ltd, set to unite this cult title with a fresh generation of readers.⁠
⁠
“I think younger people are interested in printed matter, it’s nice to make something printed in amongst this digital overload of images [on social media],” former editor @jessicagysel tells @meganhwallace. Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Courtesy of KUTT and IDEA⁠
1. Vol 1. Chloe Sevigny by Martien Mulder, 2002⁠
2. Cover⁠
3-4. Vol 3. Emmeline and Marlous by Viviane Sassen, 2003⁠
6. Vol 2. South of No North by Collier Schorr, 1995⁠
7-8. Vol 1. The Joy of Lesbian Sex, 2002⁠
⁠
#KUTT

posts.caption:
When Andrew Bolton first reached out to @sjodwyer and @karolinevitto about the @metmuseum’s new Costume Institute exhibition, both designers expected to be meeting with an assistant. But each ended up on long, searching calls with Bolton himself – a detail that underpins the seriousness with which Costume Art, the inaugural show in the Costume Institute’s newly expanded galleries, was conceived. ⁠
⁠
Pairing garments with works from across The Met’s collection – etchings, sculpture, painting – the exhibition presents the dressed body as a subject worthy of the same scrutiny as any other art historical question. For O’Dwyer and Vitto, two of the most significant independent designers working with extended sizing in fashion today, being included felt both overdue and, given the current climate, strangely timely. ⁠
⁠
In an exclusive conversation with @mad.e.leiner, the two designers discuss what it means to have this work archived, the words we still haven’t got right, and the “slow-motion car crash” of thinning they’ve both been watching for years. Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
When photographer @agnesvita went to Menorca to develop a personal project, she did so as a practice of stripping back the architecture of her everyday life, something she says felt necessary for survival. ⁠
⁠
“I don't think I was trying to escape. I was trying to find something,” she says, explaining how the trip came following a period of postnatal depression. Travelling solo as a mother might have felt controversial to some in her life, but for the photographer it was essential “to be able to listen to my own thoughts,” she explains to @laurahavlin. ⁠
⁠
As photographer Agnes Lloyd-Platt debuts her solo show Make Me Saline at @monument_london, she explains the stripped-back process that transmuted pain into hope. Read more at the link in bio 📲⁠
⁠
📸 Agnes Lloyd-Platt: Make Me Saline. Photography by Agnes Lloyd-Platt. Courtesy of @monument_london

posts.caption:
Meet the rebel tailor who brought the swinging London revolution of the 1960s to the traditional streets of Savile Row. Rising from a blue-collar background in Edgware, Tommy Nutter broke the mold when he opened Nutters in 1969.
He didn’t just make clothes; he made pop culture history. His bespoke creations include three of the four suits worn by the Beatles on the iconic Abbey Road cover, Mick Jagger’s famous three-piece wedding suit, and Elton John’s dazzling stage wear.
He wasn’t just selling clothes; he was selling an accessible, glamorous lifestyle fueled by his own magnetic personality and wit. In his own words: “The saddest thing of all is an in-between look.”
Swipe to discover the story of Tommy Nutter.

posts.caption:
UK artist @fakemink has officially debuted his full-length album, Terrified.
Built as a journey from hell to heaven, the album begins in darkness: low frequencies, ritual-like drums, and lyrics circling lust, pride, excess, and the seven deadly sins. As the record unfolds, the sound slowly opens up, moving toward the ambient stillness of its final track, “Etna.”
But Terrified is not just a sonic evolution. Across the album and its website “tales,” fakemink turns fame into a story of lost innocence: leaving Essex for LA luxury, only to find the dream becoming hollow, repetitive, and almost hellish. From empty hotel rooms to “madness and vanity,” fame is framed not as an ascension, but as a descent.
“I am just too faithful to my soil. I just had to leave it to figure that out,” he writes. “Goodbye and goodnight. We’ve been in this room too long.”
Words by Josephine Jiang
Next-Level Fashion, Daily.

Over her stellar 20-year career, @phoebejtonkin has captivated audiences. The Sydney-born, New York-based actor began acting as a teenager playing a mermaid in the series ‘H2O: Just Add Water,’ and has since achieved a dynamic array of dramatic parts, a stint in the director’s chair and a new role producing and starring in the adaptation of crime thriller, ‘The Dark Lake’. While she’s relishing the collaborative process, the actor and May 2026 cover star tells BAZAAR, acting remains her first love.“I feel really lucky to be invited into the collaborative process so early on in a project. Usually, as an actor, you turn up and you’re told, ‘This is what your set looks like, these are your fellow actors’. And with this, I get to be there from the inception of the project and be part of these decisions.”
Through the link in bio, Phoebe Tonkin reflects on her career and attraction to robust roles that “lean into the complexities of being a woman.”

“I’m attracted to things that are authentic and grounded,” @phoebejtonkin tells BAZAAR of her latest role in the cross-genre ‘Two Years Later’, a post-pandemic romantic dramedy set in Brisbane. Tonkin plays Emily, a woman with a complicated love and family life who sets out on a series of dates with Ryan, played by Brenton Thwaites, to work out if they’re destined to marry. “There was something quite relatable and ordinary about these two people who are in their 30s and trying to navigate dating, coming out of a pandemic, all the isolation and loneliness that they’ve been dealing with. And I just thought it was really real,” she shares. It was this funny, quirky love story drama that you don’t get to do often.”

In an industry where surnames are inherited currencies, Bazaar India’s April-May cover star, Tabu (@tabutiful), has operated for over three decades on a mononym. To understand her career is to understand what it meant to say yes to things the industry was not ready for and to have the instinct to recognise those things as worthy, even when no template existed for them. But she refuses to call it rebellion. “It was simply a reflection of who I am. The roles I chose, the decisions I made—they came from some similar place throughout my life. Some part of me has been completely unchanged,” she insists.

posts.caption:
This week, Chanel reported its annual results for 2025. Revenue rose 2 percent to $19.3 billion, defying a luxury downturn.
But the number that caught the industry’s attention wasn’t the top line — it was the acceleration. In the second half of the year, Chanel’s sales grew by high single digits across every category and every region — and this was before designs by the house’s new artistic director, Matthieu Blazy, had even arrived in stores. The excitement alone was enough to change the trajectory.
The momentum has two sources. One is Blazy, whose runway debut last October has sparked what the industry is calling “Blazymania”, a level of excitement Chanel hasn’t seen in years. The other is the leadership of chief executive Leena Nair, who has spent her tenure investing heavily in Chanel’s retail network, its manufacturing, and its people — building the foundations that allowed a creative spark to catch fire.
Two years before they would work together at Chanel, Blazy and Nair spoke at #BoFVOICES 2023. Separately they each made the case for craft, people and long-term thinking over speed and scale.
This week on The BoF Podcast, we revisit their talks to understand how their synergistic philosophies are powering Chanel’s newfound momentum.
🎙️ Listen now #linkinbio #BoFPodcast
📷 Getty Images

posts.caption:
Hi this is @ImranAmed, welcome to my weekly letter.
On the surface, there was plenty to celebrate at the Financial Times’ Business of Luxury Summit this week. Delphine Arnault and Jonathan Anderson marked one year of their joint effort to re-energise Dior. Stéphane de La Faverie, chief executive of The Estée Lauder Companies, underscored the beauty conglomerate’s nine consecutive months of growth after four years of decline. And Victoria Beckham said both her fashion and beauty divisions are now profitable after almost two decades of losses.
But beneath the public relations spin, a reckoning is underway as the luxury industry struggles to recover from a punishing downturn in demand.
When asked what is the greatest risk facing the luxury industry, Bain’s Claudia D’Arpizio didn’t cite tariffs, the war in the Middle East or slow growth in China, but rising inequality: the risk that luxury goods are becoming markers of exclusion rather than totems of aspiration.
This is a dynamic The Business of Fashion has been tracking for at least a decade. That risk has only increased amid the rise of America’s tech centibillionaires, Asia’s family empires and the global hedge fund class. Contrary to popular belief, “old money” doesn’t drive the luxury sector: The majority of luxury growth comes from aspirants and the upwardly mobile — you get a better life and you mark the occasion and signal your newfound status by buying something beautiful. But when wealth polarisation becomes so extreme that the ladder itself feels broken, aspiration melts away. People stop admiring the wealthy, and start resenting them.
According to research by Bain, the luxury market lost 50 million customers between 2022 and 2024, shrinking from 400 million to 350 million globally, amid macroeconomic gloom and extravagant price increases. “Very important customers” — the top 2 percent of spenders — now account for 45 percent of all luxury purchases, up from 35 percent in 2021, while millions of shoppers have walked away.
So, how did we end up here? Read my full letter at the #linkinbio

posts.caption:
It’s a deal few would have expected.
Shein, the ultra-fast fashion juggernaut that’s popular with Gen Z, has acquired Everlane, the fallen direct-to-consumer Millennial darling whose well-priced wardrobe staples, wrapped in the promise of “radical transparency,” were once synonymous with conscious consumerism.
The sale — which Everlane CEO Alfred Chang announced Friday, confirming a report that broke last week in Puck — follows a tumultuous period for San Francisco-based Everlane. In recent years, the label struggled with public scandal, mounting debt and a shifting retail landscape, ultimately prompting its majority owner, private equity giant L Catterton, to offload the asset.
The financial terms of the transaction were not disclosed, but reports in Puck and The Information said the deal valued Everlane at $100 million, a fraction of the $600 million it was reportedly worth at the time of the Catterton investment.
Read the full story by BoF’s @kittykewlcat #linkinbio

posts.caption:
At its Cruise show in April, Chanel stretched the very definition of a shoe.
The French megabrand sent models down the runway wearing a soleless sandal, with a slim heel covering tethered to their ankles, leaving their arches and toes exposed. The look quickly went viral, sparking hot debate among those who loved it, hated it — and were just plain confused.
Chanel’s may be the most boundary-pushing, but it’s just the latest in a line of experimental shoes to break onto the market. The trend has been building for years as out-there styles like toe shoes, mesh flats and barely-there sandals dominated the summer trend cycle.
Read more on how weird shoes became a business strategy. #linkinbio

Richemont sales soared in the last quarter, reflecting the ongoing strength of its jewellery houses Cartier and Van Cleef & Arpels, with an added boost from local shoppers in Japan.
Sales for the three months to the end of March reached 5.4 billion Swiss francs ($6.9 billion), up 13 percent at constant currency exchange, outpacing analyst expectations of 9 percent growth.
Sales in Japan were up 28 percent, driven by strong local demand, which more than offset a decline in spending from tourists.

posts.caption:
The clean girl is dead, and the flat lay is officially out. “The top of a girl’s dresser says so much about her,” says Avery-Claire. Growing up, “while other girls were watching Hannah Montana and studying her outfits, I was studying her bedside table.”
That souvenir keychain tossed beside an old cigarette, the hair ribbon from a night out, the random knickknacks collecting in a dish on your bedside table — they reveal more about you than you’d think.
Everyone’s “cluster” feels like a form of interior design for who they are. Maybe it’s something you’ve held onto since childhood, or a matchbox from a first date you can’t quite bring yourself to throw away. Clusters aren’t clutter — they’re intentional. And @girlswhocluster is turning the art of collected objects into its own aesthetic language.
Swipe through to see some of her favorite clusters, and the anatomy of a classic cluster.

posts.caption:
When she was four, the artist Widline Cadet (@widline_ ) was separated from her mother for six years as she emigrated from Haiti to New York to pursue a better life for her family.
At 10 years old, Cadet reunited with her mother in New York, but as she grew into adulthood, she realized that she didn't know her well at all. Nor did Cadet have a larger sense of her family, the ancestral threads that weave back through time.
Photographs of her own childhood and family were scarce. Her mother didn't have a picture of her own mother. Memories faded with each passing year.
For nearly a decade, Cadet has been crafting her own "living archive," exploring the diasporic experience and the elusiveness of memory. The largest presentation of her work to date is on view at @milwaukeeart.
📸: Widline Cadet

At @festivaldecannes on Wednesday, supermodel @bellahadid attended the premiere of Antonin Baudry's new Charles de Gaulle biopic, "La Bataille de Gaulle," in a form-fitting ivory gown by Schiaparelli.
From its trompe l'œil lace embroidery to the dark brooch holding everything together, the French couture house's inspiration was clear: the crochet floral gown that late style icon Jane Birkin wore to the Union of the Artists gala in Paris in 1969.
In contrast to Hadid's picture-perfect styling, Birkin's outfit had resulted from a last-minute wardrobe fix — one that epitomized her gloriously blithe approach to fashion.
Believing her neckline to be too high, the English socialite decided to wear her @emiliopucci dress backwards. In doing so, she turned a backless gown into a daringly plunging one.
Almost six decades later, moments of such sartorial insouciance have all but disappeared from the red carpet. But let this not be a criticism of Hadid, her longtime stylist mimicuttrell or the gown's creator, @schiaparelli creative director @danielroseberry, who together created an outfit befitting of the moment.
📸 : Lyvans Boolaky/Getty Images/Samir Hussein/WireImage/Keystone-France/Gamma-Keystone/

posts.caption:
On Monday evening, it took auction house @christiesinc just 40 minutes to sell more than $630 million in art, smashing records for both the painter Jackson Pollock and sculptor Constantin Brancusi. Later in the night, it rounded things off with yet more broken records and another $490 million.
The first part of the $1.1-billion New York sale featured 16 works from S.I. Newhouse's collection. It marked the fourth time Christie's has brought a group of works to market from the late media giant, who owned @condenast, as well as large slice of America's local newspapers and broadcast stations.
But it was the first time that the auction house tapped @nicolekidman for a promotional video, filming her free-flowing encounter with the golden Brancusi bust "Danaïde," which was poised to be one of the night's star lots. The video was based on a 1930s Man Ray film of fellow surrealist Lee Miller, per Christie's, and while it didn't quite take off in the same way as Kidman's viral AMC ad, it did once again prove her commitment to any and all roles.
Monday's sales at Christie's also included three masterworks from the collection of former Museum of Modern Art president Agnes Gund, which set a new $98.4 million record for Mark Rothko, while Sotheby's offered up treasures worth $166.3 million from the late art dealer Robert Mnuchin last week.
📸 : Hunter Abrams/Courtesy Christie's

posts.caption:
Rejecting fashion school doesn’t automatically make someone a stronger designer. I don’t really understand why “self taught” has become something people wear as a badge of honor in fashion.
School gave me construction knowledge, design development skills, sourcing experience, technical understanding, and a broader understanding of how clothing actually functions. Not everything resonated with me and I still take classes now because learning never stops. I am currently studying shoe design because I want to continue expanding my practice.
You do not need formal education to become a designer, but I think people sometimes romanticize the idea of skipping foundations entirely. Understanding construction, pattern making, fit, textiles, and design process doesn’t limit creativity. It gives you more tools.
I don’t design with standard techniques now, but knowing the rules gave me the ability to intentionally bend and break them. The foundation made the work stronger.
The goal shouldn’t be school versus self taught. The goal should be becoming the strongest designer you can become.

A joyous quality runs throughout Honey’s work, which documents women’s experiences at various stages of life: capturing the transition from childhood to adulthood with tenderness, rich colours and a kind of hazy, nostalgic glow. ⁠

posts.caption:
This summer’s style mood feels less clean and much more personal. The best outfits right now look slightly chaotic, undone and thrown together in the best way, tiny shorts with oversized shirts, ballet flats with sportswear, messy layering, and vintage accessoires.
Sleaze and old runway styling, but styled in a more playful way. From Kate Moss-denim micro shorts and slouchy belts to colorful tights, fitted tanks, embellished shorts and oversized graphic tees, the focus is less about looking “perfect” and more about having character.
Even the more dressed-up looks still feel authentic. Smudged sunglasses, worn-in denim, oversized bags, ballet shoes, tiny gym shorts or random statement pieces make the outfits feel human instead of overstyled. This summer is really about mixing glamour with casualness, looking like you didn’t try too hard while still creating a fun silhouette and attitude.

posts.caption:
In Athletic Girl for Vogue Italia August 2016, Steven Klein turns fashion photography into a staged power fantasy. The editorial plays with dominance, sexuality and control, placing Lara Stone at the center of every image more like a character than a model. She doesn’t just wear the clothes, she completely controls the atmosphere of the story.
What makes the editorial so memorable is how fully it leans into that idea. The exaggerated poses, glossy styling and theatrical tension give the images a very specific energy that still feels striking years later. Lara Stone brings this raw, almost intimidating presence to the story, while Patti Wilson’s styling pushes everything further into this hyper-glamorous, slightly surreal world.
It’s the kind of editorial that understands fashion photography should create a mood, not just present clothes.

posts.caption:
What makes the Zara x Bad Bunny collaboration feel so strange is the contradiction behind it. Over the past years, Benito built an image around political awareness, authenticity and cultural resistance. He positioned himself as an artist who speaks for marginalized communities and distances himself from the polished corporate machinery dominating both music and fashion.
That is exactly why this partnership feels so cynical.
Zara is not just another fashion brand. It is one of the largest fast fashion corporations in the world, built on mass production, aggressive consumption and the commercialization of identities. Watching an artist whose image is so closely tied to rebellion and anti establishment messaging suddenly become the face of that machine inevitably raises questions about how performative celebrity activism really is once global corporations get involved.
And the collaboration becomes even harder to defend creatively because the collection itself feels painfully uninspired. Oversized basics, washed tones, predictable silhouettes and a campaign aesthetic that looks indistinguishable from regular Zara menswear. There is nothing here that reflects the risk taking or visual boldness that made Bad Bunny culturally interesting in the first place.
Without Benito’s name attached to it, the collection would most likely disappear into the endless cycle of forgettable Zara drops within a week.
Of course, it would also be dishonest to pretend most celebrities would reject a collaboration like this. Fashion has always been tied to capitalism and almost every major artist eventually becomes absorbed into the same corporate structures they once appeared to criticize.
But with Bad Bunny, the contradiction feels especially glaring because so much of his appeal came from the idea that he was different from exactly this kind of system.

posts.caption:
This collection understood clothing as character.
David Koma Pre-Fall 2025 has that thing where the clothes don’t just look good, they immediately tell you who the person wearing them might be.
There’s a lot happening here: sheer bodysuits, fur collars, red tights, sharp heels, leather, denim, long black silhouettes, strange proportions. But it still feels controlled. Nothing looks random, even when the shapes are dramatic.
The collection plays with a very specific kind of glamour. Not soft, not romantic, not too clean. More like someone dressing up to be watched, but still keeping a bit of distance.
The fur pieces make the body look framed. The red looks feel almost graphic. The sheer pieces keep things light, but never weak. Even the denim and leather moments still belong to the same world.
That’s what makes it interesting. It’s not just a set of nice looks. It feels like a cast of characters.
Credits:
Collection: David Koma Pre-Fall 2025
Designer: David Koma

posts.caption:
The long tailored coat has always belonged to people who understand presence.
Before it became a fashion statement, it was a garment of function: protection, structure, authority. Military greatcoats, officer coats, riding coats, city overcoats, all of them were built around the same idea: length creates command. A short jacket moves with the body. A long coat changes the body.
That is the power of tailoring. It doesn’t simply cover; it edits. The shoulder is sharpened, the torso is lengthened, the walk becomes slower, more deliberate. A well-cut coat gives the wearer architecture before they even enter a room.
In fashion, the long coat has moved through many lives. It has been masculine, cinematic, severe, romantic, corporate, rebellious. On women, especially, it carries a particular tension: the language of menswear, reworked into something colder, softer, more dangerous. The best versions never feel decorative. They feel controlled.
What makes a long tailored coat good is not only the fabric, but the discipline behind it. The weight of the wool, the fall of the lapel, the placement of the button, the line from shoulder to hem. Too little structure and it becomes a robe. Too much and it becomes costume. The perfect coat sits somewhere in between: precise, but not stiff; dramatic, but not theatrical.
Today, its appeal feels even sharper. In a fashion landscape obsessed with novelty, the long tailored coat offers something rarer: permanence. It doesn’t rely on trend. It relies on proportion, fabric, and attitude.
A long coat does not complete an outfit.
It becomes the outfit.

For decades, diamond retail success in the US was driven by one formula: stock more, sell more, discount more.
That formula is changing.
Today, consumers are buying fewer pieces, but spending more on purchases that feel meaningful, personal, and worth the price.
With the US accounting for 53% of global diamond jewellery demand, this shift is sending a clear message to the entire industry.
What this means for jewellers, manufacturers, and exporters:
The winners will not be those with the largest inventory.
They will be the ones who can create stronger emotional value, build consumer trust, and position both natural and lab-grown diamonds with clarity.
Value over volume is the new rule of diamond retail.

posts.caption:
For decades, diamond retail success was measured by one thing: volume.
More inventory.
More promotions.
More units sold.
That model is being rewritten.
The US, which accounts for 53% of global diamond jewellery demand — is shifting toward a value-driven retail model where consumers are buying fewer pieces but spending more per purchase.
Here’s what’s changing:
💎 Finished jewellery is outperforming loose diamonds
📈 Average ticket sizes are rising sharply
🔬 Lab-grown diamonds are dominating entry-level demand
✨ Natural diamonds are being repositioned as premium luxury
🛍 Storytelling, traceability and trust are driving conversions
The message for manufacturers, wholesalers and retailers is clear:
Success in 2026 won’t come from selling more.
It will come from selling smarter.
If your product offers stronger design, emotional significance and a compelling story, consumers are willing to pay for it.

posts.caption:
We could go see Boop!
Quinta Brunson is set to develop and star as Betty Boop in a new film by her production company Fifth Chance Productions, alongside creator Max Fleischer’s grandson Mark Fleischer and Fleischer Studios.
According to the film’s press release, the story will trace Betty’s origin and evolution through Max Fleischer’s perspective, digging into the “relationship between the artist and his creation as he navigates the creative and commercial pressures of building one of the world’s first animated icons, particularly as that icon begins to take on a life of its own.”
Online reactions to Brunson stepping into the role are split. Some fans see the two-time Emmy winner as a perfect fit for the character’s reboot, while others are questioning her casting.
Betty Boop’s influence and origins have long-sparked debate. While Fleischer claimed to have based the character on a dog, singer Helen Kane famously sued the studio, arguing Boop was modeled after her. In court, the studio pointed the character’s iconic baby voice to Black performers of the 1920s, including Baby Esther, a popular child star.
Betty’s roots have been debatable for decades, but either way, she’s a fictional character and Brunson is a great choice for the legendary diva!
Swipe through for more of her fascinating history.
#bettyboop #quintabrunson #film #history #animation

After years of soft neutrals, skin tints, and “no-makeup makeup” dominating the algorithm, Marc Jacobs Beauty is relaunching on its own terms with statement shades and party balloon-inspired packaging.
The brand first debuted in 2013 and quickly reached cult status before disappearing in 2021 after its partnership with Kendo ended. Now it returns under a new license with Coty Inc., re-entering a market that has fundamentally shifted.
While “clean girl” minimalism has dominated beauty since the brand shuttered, the cultural pendulum seems to be swinging towards color and maximalism, and away from former mega-brands like Glossier.
Marc Jacobs Beauty is returning with saturated pigments, high-gloss finishes, and sculptural packaging in a relaunch Jacobs calls a celebration of “creativity and self-expression.”
The rollout includes seven hero products priced from $26 to $42, reviving fan favorites like its best-selling gel liners in 21 shades, alongside new products like a multi-use blush stick.
Whether this return meaningfully disrupts the beauty category remains to be seen, but it’s clearly not trying to blend in.
Marc Jacobs Beauty drops May 28 on marcjacobs.com, followed by a June 1 release at Sephora.
#marcjacobs #beauty #makeup #marcjacobsbeauty #sephora

posts.caption:
On May 19th, Jonathan Andic was arrested in connection to the death of his father Isak Andic, the billionaire founder of Mango, who died in 2024 after slipping into a ravine on a Montserrat hiking trail. Authorities have outlined new evidence against Jonathan, prompting his arrest as a homicide suspect.
Jonathan, the only witness to his father’s death, claimed that he was a few meters ahead on the hiking trail when his father paused to take photos of the scenery. He told emergency services that he heard the fall, but did not see it occur. He also claimed that his relationship with his father was good, despite known tensions around Jonathan’s business failings at Mango in 2015.
However, new evidence shows that Isak’s cell phone was found in his pocket at the time of his death, and no photos appear on his camera roll of the hike. As well, the autopsy report found no scuffs on Isak’s hands, suggesting that he fell backward, not forward, into the ravine. Examination of Isak’s shoes also showed that the soles were worn down “deliberately” to create the appearance of a slip. Jonathan was also found to have visited the hiking trail multiple times shortly before his father’s death, causing investigators to suspect premeditation.
Most importantly, Jonathan’s tense relationship with his father was illuminated by Estefanía Knuth, the professional golfer and long-term girlfriend of Isak (who happens to be in a legal dispute with Isak’s children for €70m of the inheritance.) Knuth’s claims are supported by WhatsApp messages between Jonathan and Isak which reveal the son’s “obsession with money” and his demands for a living inheritance, which Isak was “forced” to agree to.
Finally, in 2024, Jonathan learned that Isak had planned to set up a “foundation to help people in need.” WhatsApp messages show a shift in the son’s attitude towards his father, causing him to express a desire to reconcile their differences. Thus, Jonathan invited Isak on the hike in Montserrat, just the two of them, to “talk alone.” Wanting to recover his relationship with his son, Isak accepted the invitation that would lead to his untimely death, and the foundation was never realized.

According to the president, the new ballroom is also a new security flex for additional construction underneath it. “The ballroom becomes a shield for everything that you see here,” he said, describing it as “the one opportunity for the military to do something.” Guess the bunker theories were right all along.

posts.caption:
As AI looms over job prospects, it’s no surprise it has become an unwelcome topic at this year’s college commencements… well, at least for new grads anyway.
Two viral videos emerged over the past week showing graduation speakers gloating about the future of AI. Gloria Caulfield, a real estate exec, went viral after she looked visibly shocked as students booed her May 8 commencement speech at the University of Central Florida after comparing AI to the industrial revolution. “What happened?,” she asked. “I struck a nerve.”
That same energy popped up again at the University of Arizona, where former Google CEO Eric Schmidt delivered a similar message last Friday, but even more aggressively. “If you don’t care about science that’s okay,” he stated, “because AI is gonna touch everything else as well.” Schmidt reportedly anticipated backlash from students following allegations of rape and sexual harassment, and surveillance, filed in a lawsuit last November.
Online, the comments are basically echoing the same vibe. “Aren’t commencement addresses supposed to be motivating?,” one Threads user wrote. “Why do they keep sending AI simps to college graduations to brag to them about how AI is going to steal their jobs?,” another asked.
At Glendale Community College, dozens of graduate names were reportedly skipped and/or mispronounced by an AI reader replacing traditional announcers.
College president Dr. Tiffany Hernandez laughed off the tech issue, saying, “Each of you were able to walk the stage and get a picture, which is what I would hope is the most meaningful,” she said. “I am so sorry. There’s plenty of opportunities, I hope, to take some really good pictures and to celebrate you with your loved ones as well.”
Maybe AI just isn’t needed in a tradition that’s been doing fine for hundreds of years without it.

posts.caption:
Everlane has officially sold to Shein, the fast-fashion giant, in light of the company’s $90m debt burden.
The capsule-wardrobe retailer was founded in 2011, promising sustainability and “radical transparency” in their pricing and supply chain. Their target customer was a coming-of-age millennial who was eager for affordable fashion that aligned with their value systems. For years, Everlane remained the de facto workplace-casual retailer for that very customer, offering simple, non-branded, trend resistant options which supposedly didn’t come from sweatshops and took a bullheaded approach to cutting carbon emissions.
You could say that Everlane and brands like it defined the 2010s, to some extent. Everlane was a leader in a decade of optimistic retailers, each of which sold its own tailored promise of creating a better world through DTC sales; Reformation sold ethical occasion dresses, All Birds sold sneakers made of recycled materials, Outdoor Voices sold inclusive athleisure, and so on. The millennial utopia was, it seems, an ethical value proposition that consumers and investors could get behind.
But now, many of these brands are falling apart under the crushing truth of capitalism: Ethical consumption may not be as realistic on the large-scale as we’d hoped it could be. Everlane is, afterall, being acquired by the very force of retail that it was supposed to replace, one that would sooner churn out desirable styles and exploit consumers’ growing interest in hyper-trends and luxury than worry over the environment or supply chain problems like slave labour.
What does that mean for customers who relied on Everlane to provide a cohesive wardrobe sans effort, anxiety, or guilt? One Reddit post asks: “What are we buying/wearing to replace Everlane?” The responses are bleak, offering little in terms of replacements and much in terms of disappointment.
“I’m just so sick of everything. I just want quality clothing that’s not made from child labor and has ethical/sustainable production lines,” one user said.
Can a brand like that exist anymore? Or is the dream of the 2010s dead?

What do Sarah Michelle Gellar, Tom Holland, and reportedly, Bruce Lee, all have in common? They’ve all used EMS workouts to get fit.
This celeb-loved workout is a relatively new trend in which electrodes are placed directly onto muscles, which “bypasses the brain and spinal cord and causes a contraction of the target muscle,” as one physical therapist describes it. While wearing a full-body suit, users can stimulate their quads, abs, hamstrings, biceps, and back all at the same time. It’s hard to tell how much is hyperbole, but according to #ChrissyTeigen, her EMS experience made 20 minutes of exercise feel equivalent to four hours of working out.
Sound too good to be true? At the link in bio, @kathleenhou examines the benefits of electrical muscle stimulation. And explore the rest of our Working It Out package, which investigates modern fitness and its increasing impact on our everyday lives.

posts.caption:
In 2014—the same year she won her Oscar—@lupitanyongo was diagnosed with fibroids.
At the time, she decided to get a myomectomy, a procedure to remove them, but they came back, unbeknown to her. “No alarm was sounded until I learned that I had 50,” she tells ELLE. “Again, I was told, ‘You can either live with it or get another surgery.’ And I thought to myself, ‘How has my fibroid burden changed, and the options you have for me have not? This doesn’t sound right.’”
Feeling a mix of agony and frustration at the lack of research and funding, she decided to go public with her diagnosis: “I thought to myself, ‘If I’m going through this, that means so many other people are going through this, too.’” She has recently partnered with the @foundationwomenshealth, and earlier this year launched the Make Fibroids Count campaign to fund a research grant in her name. Speaking up has liberated me in so many ways,” she says, “and I feel very rewarded by this work.”
For ELLE’s Summer 2026 issue, Nyong’o graces one of four covers featuring the women of ‘The Odyssey.’ Read her full cover story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
“Hi Shakaila we’re xyz producers and we’d love to make a tv show with you” I’ve had a handful of those conversations over the years and they quickly go from 🤩 to 🫩
I used to feel disheartened until I looked around and saw so many examples of people not waiting for permission and instead deciding to get up and make shit happen! So that’s what I decided to do, turn this decades long dream into a reality! I pulled in every favour I had! Turned my friends into producers and show runners, begged and borrowed and you know what, it turned out pretty damn good if I do say so myself 😅
This black history month especially, I’m inspired by the talented Black British women who have shown me that it’s possible to make a mark on the small screen no matter the limitations!
I absolutely loved makeover shows growing up! But as I got older I saw how they were more brutal than beautiful!
Today, fashion psychology shows us just how deeply our clothes impact our mental health, identity, and confidence.
That’s why I’m so excited to share The Wearapy Show my new makeover series that blends fashion psychology, emotional healing and real style transformations.
We help everyday people reclaim their wardrobes after major life shifts: grief, motherhood, identity struggles, and more.
With a team of experts and no judgment, just science-backed guidance and love.
Episodes 1 and 2 launch 26th October on the Fashion is Psychology YouTube Channel. Subscribe now and follow the journey.

posts.caption:
HUGE NEWS: Good On You has officially rated over 7,000 brands 🎉 This is a major accomplishment, as even more brands—from the small and indie through the large and luxury—are in our Directory.⁠
⁠
What goes into ratings? 🤔 A lot, actually: we have a rigorous, comprehensive system analysing up to 1,000 data points across 100 key issues to arrive at our lowest score (“We Avoid”) to our highest score (“Great”). Ratings are based solely on a brand’s transparent, public reporting that we pair with credible third-party indices, certifications, and standards.⁠
⁠
How do we select brands to rate? Well…with your help 🥹 Many of our ratings come through community requests, shared in comments and messages, which helps us understand which brands to prioritise to meet this moment. Additionally, our retail and ecommerce partners along with brands request ratings too, which is a key means to apply change to the industry from within.⁠
⁠
Another item to celebrate here? Of these rated brands, 1,000 have received our two highest scores, “Good” and “Great”. This is very exciting news, representative of how more brands are doing considering their impact on workers, the planet, and animals. (Even if that also means…6000 brands have a lot more to do to reduce their impact.)⁠
⁠
Here’s to continuing to keep people like you informed while supporting more sustainable brands that are making the difference! Cheers 🥂 Explore our 7,000 ratings at the link too ✨⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #FastFashion #ConsciousConsumer #EthicalConsumer #SustainableStyle #ConsciousStyle #GoodOnYou #GoodOnYouApp #EthicalFashion⁠

posts.caption:
No one wants to wear plastic. And yet: so many of us are 😢⁠
⁠
From a report we conducted last year, echoed by the 2024 Circularity Gap Report, the vast majority of materials being made are synthetics—which means most clothes being made come from synthetics 😵⁠
⁠
Synthetics do not biodegrade and shed microplastics when washed. They are an environmental disaster, all of which is propelled by being fast fashion’s material of choice. What you wear reflects your values and plastic is likely *not* the vibe you’re trying to exude 🚮 Visit the link to learn more about this issue and about better alternatives ↑⁠
⁠
#Fashion #OOTD #ZaraStyle #HMStyle #SHEINGals

This is why it's highly likely your clothes are plastic. Visit the link to learn more about better materials and the dangers of plastic clothes'.

posts.caption:
A mantra to hold onto: don’t let shopping define who you are 🕊️⁠
⁠
Too often, meaning is measured in what you own and what you buy, an “it” product or the pressure to “get something” seen as a cultural communication tool. That’s not true 🙂‍↔️⁠
⁠
If you’re having trouble quitting “stuff”, here are a few tips the Good On You team abide by—⁠
⁠
🌅 Consider the future, for you, the object, and everything else around you. Is this item essential? How will it age with you? Understanding its place in your life is a key to figuring out a need versus a want.⁠
⁠
🧐 Keep an inventory of what you own, to avoid duplicative purchases and needless accumulation. Understanding what you have is helpful in saying no to things you already own, especially when you don’t have capacity.⁠
⁠
⏲️ Set limits for yourself, to ensure you’re only buying one thing a month or only spending a certain amount of money. Better yet? Challenge yourself to buy nothing and to save money, placing an emphasis on future opportunities that can improve your life beyond the “now” of a single purchase.⁠
⁠
💭 Understand your temptations, be they pressure from push notifications or influencers you follow or your friend group. This knowledge can help you manage environments that push you to needlessly shop.⁠
⁠
How do you avoid the pressure to shop? Share in the comments, then explore a story sharing advice on quitting over-shopping ↑⁠
⁠
#SustainableFashion #Fashion #Shopping #Sale #Zara⁠

posts.caption:
For all their coding genius and super-efficient microchips, tech giants still can’t quite crack swag.⁠
⁠
So when Silicon Valley wants to sell camera-equipped AI glasses, it calls in fashion people to make them look less painfully tech-brained. Meta figured this out first, partnering with Ray-Ban in 2019 to remake its iconic Wayfarers and later extending the formula to Oakley’s sporty, accidentally stylish performance shades. Now Google and Samsung are entering the race, with Apple reportedly not far behind.⁠
⁠
Instead of linking with eyewear heavyweights, @google and @samsung tapped @gentlemonster, the South Korean label responsible for some of fashion’s strangest and best futuristic frames. The result is a surprisingly sleek pair of all-black cat-eye smart glasses arriving this fall. Samsung supplies the discreet built-in cameras while Google adds Gemini AI features like navigation, translation, and messaging. Basically, they do all the usual smart-glasses tricks but finally look like something people might actually want to wear.

posts.caption:
No century-old workwear brand hits quite like @carhartt. This is the label Tupac wore head-to-toe, the source of those perfectly thrashed hoodies that’ve become menswear grails, and the blueprint for artisanal brands obsessed with recreating the wear-and-tear magic of vintage blue-collar uniforms. What’s wild is that through all the hype, Carhartt never really flinched. It still makes sturdy, affordable gear for actual workers first. But its new Crafted Series feels like the closest thing to an American-made Carhartt fashion line: archival reproductions cut from heavier fabrics, finished with thoughtful details like old-school heart-logo buttons and contrast stitching that normal Carhartt would never bother with.⁠
⁠
This latest drop might be one of our favorite because it leans into painter uniforms without losing that grounded, workwear honesty. The undyed cotton drill, navy reinforcement stitching, and little flashes of red thread give the pieces just enough personality without tipping into costume territory. Honestly, if you showed me the set without context, assume it was a Carhartt WIP release or some expensive Japanese reinterpretation of workwear history. But that’s the charm here: it’s still the real American Carhartt, just sharpening its edges a little.

posts.caption:
Tension is heavy on the mind at the ongoing Venice Biennale, yet in ‘Liberation Space’ at the Korean Pavilion, it’s the animating force.⁠
⁠
In our latest ‘Visits,’ we caught up with Hyeree Ro (@hyereemro), one of the artists repping South Korea, at her studio in Brooklyn. Anchored in dreams for coexistence, Ro’s project, ‘Bearing’ channels the quiet acts of care and endurance as a “living, breathing monument.”⁠
⁠
Her work has a way of holding you, a nest-like “embracing,” she explains. “But you also need to leave your nest and go out into the world.” There’s a mesmeric magic to watching an artist lock into her craft. And for Ro, it’s game time.⁠
⁠
Read the full interview at the link in our bio.⁠
⁠
Credit: Donghwan Kam / Orlando Thompson / Hyunjung Rhee / Courtesy of the artist and the @korean_pavilion, 2026 Venice Biennale.

posts.caption:
There are people who wear time, and then there are people who define it. @palaktiwarii belongs to the second kind.✨
She carries something rare, a weight that isn’t borrowed from the past, but isn’t chasing the future either. It simply exists, with the kind of quiet confidence that doesn’t need to announce itself.
In this frame, legacy and the now collapse into each other. @guesswatches on her wrist, intent in her eyes, highlighting the “Main Character” hunger behind the gold hardware; this is a story of a woman who isn’t just wearing the accessories of success, but is actively forging them. ⌚️
The Architecture of Time isn’t a concept. It’s a posture. A way of owning every second without letting a single one own you.
@palaktiwarii doesn’t follow the tempo. She sets it. And right now, she’s setting it for all of us. 🫶🏻

posts.caption:
Introducing our latest cover star, @katehudson!
It’s been over 25 years since Kate Hudson earned her first Oscar nomination for her breakout role as Penny Lane in ‘Almost Famous,’ and she’s been shaping the cultural moment ever since. Despite her success—and her roots in Hollywood royalty—she’s remained remarkably grounded, continuing to speak about the industry with a genuine sense of gratitude.
“I just really want to make movies that people love,” Hudson tells L’OFFICIEL. “It’s really simple for me. I feel a responsibility as someone that loves this business, and grew up in it, and really wanted to make movies. I hope that I can make movies and shows that people really want to see.”
That, of course, has always been Hudson’s strength. Fresh off another Oscar nomination for her role in ‘Song Sung Blue,’ she’s now turning her attention back to television with Season 2 of ‘Running Point.’ In addition to executive producing the Netflix series, Hudson stars as Isla Gordon, a character loosely inspired by Los Angeles Lakers owner and president Jeanie Buss. While the role has prompted comparisons to Hudson’s own upbringing, she’s quick to dismiss that narrative. “Jeanie has had to maintain the most important team in sports history, and on top of that, the money and the franchise and everything that goes with it,” she says. “For me, we’re just actors. People are either gonna like what we do or they’re not.”
Read our full interview with Hudson about the collaborative nature of film, her creative process, and growing up in the industry at the link in bio.

@KateHudson has taken on a wide range of genres, but she’s still focused on pushing herself even further. When we asked Hudson what she still hopes to achieve in her career, she didn’t point to accolades—just a desire to keep pursuing roles that scare her, the same approach she brings to everything she does. ⁠
⁠
“I enter every experience like it’s new, like I don’t know how it’s going to unfold,” she explains. “I come in doing my part, which is understanding what makes me feel confident in the role and the character, but I do that so that when I’m there, I can be flexible, not rigid.” Read Hudson’s full interview at the link in our bio. ⁠

posts.caption:
French artist @JR turns #PontNeuf into a monumental “quarry” in the middle of Paris. The 400-year-old bridge now appears as a jagged rock formation, rising into a trompe-l’oeil illusion that will eventually tower nearly 18 meters above the city.
Titled ‘La Caverne du Pont Neuf,’ the installation is conceived as an homage to Christo and Jeanne-Claude’s 1985 work ‘The Pont Neuf Wrapped,’ reinterpreting the landmark through a new environmental illusion. Using photographic printing in tones of white, black, and gray, the structure creates a textured stone effect that plays with perception—already giving passersby a preview of the full transformation, while setting the stage for the interior to come. Read more on the installation at the link in bio.

#JR #pontneuf #lacavernedupontneuf

Jacob & Co. continues to blur the line between watchmaking, sculpture, and fantasy with the Astronomia “Gates of Olympus.”
Priced at $1.4 million, the one-of-one creation draws inspiration from Greek mythology, featuring Zeus, Olympus, celestial movement, and a fully mechanical miniature world built directly into the timepiece itself.
Inside the 18K rose gold case sits a triple-axis tourbillon, over 9 carats of baguette diamonds, and an intricate Olympus scene designed as part of the movement.
More than a watch, the piece reflects how modern haute horology is increasingly moving toward collectible art and immersive storytelling.

posts.caption:
@Balenciaga dropped its Spring Summer 2026 preview collection in Mumbai last night, and the energy in the room felt different.
With Fatima Sana Sheikh, Bobby Deol, Mouni Roy, Sanya Malhotra, Malavika Mohanan, and Krithi Shetty in attendance, it felt less like a formal showcase and more like a real moment around fashion. The room was sharp, observant, and engaged.
Fatima Sana Shaikh said it best: “Balenciaga always has a chic approach and the brand is now heading towards it even more aggressively.” And that tone carried through the evening. Nothing felt loud for attention, yet everything commanded it.
What stood out was how people responded. There was curiosity, closer inspection, and genuine interest in the details. Some silhouettes felt familiar but more refined, while others brought a quiet sense of drama without trying too hard.
That is when a collection works.
Not when it demands attention, but when it earns it.
@balenciaga #Balenciaga

posts.caption:
Ralph Lauren has officially crossed $8 billion in annual revenue for the first time in the brand’s history, marking a major milestone for the American luxury giant.
The company reported $8.11 billion in revenue for fiscal year 2026, driven by strong global demand, major growth in China, and continued momentum across direct-to-consumer retail and digital sales. Asia emerged as the strongest-performing region, with China alone recording more than 50% growth during the Lunar New Year period.
Under CEO Patrice Louvet, Ralph Lauren has spent the last several years repositioning itself through elevated branding, younger consumers, social media expansion, and stronger full-price luxury positioning. While several luxury houses continue facing slower demand globally, Ralph Lauren has managed to strengthen both profitability and cultural relevance simultaneously.
The milestone further reinforces Ralph Lauren’s position as one of the few heritage American fashion brands successfully competing at the highest level of global luxury today.
@RalphLauren #RalphLauren #Luxurious

posts.caption:
Louis Vuitton’s Cruise 2027 show transformed New York’s historic Frick Collection into one of fashion’s most talked-about runways of the year.
Presented by Nicolas Ghesquière, the collection explored the contrast between uptown elegance and downtown New York energy through sculptural tailoring, oversized silhouettes, metallic textures, and bold Keith Haring-inspired graphics pulled from the Maison’s archives. The show also marked the first-ever fashion presentation inside the Frick Collection’s historic galleries.
Beyond the runway, the presentation launched a three-year partnership between Louis Vuitton and The Frick Collection, reinforcing the luxury house’s growing focus on fashion, art, architecture, and cultural preservation.
Online, the collection sparked mixed reactions. While many praised the artistic direction and Keith Haring references, others called it one of Louis Vuitton’s most polarising Cruise collections in recent years.
Images: @louisvuitton

#ManifestCover
There is a quiet power to Rukmini Vasanth (@rukmini_vasanth). One that doesn’t seek attention, yet holds it effortlessly.
For Manifest’s May–June cover, she steps into an imaginative world where temple jewellery meets contemporary silhouettes, blurring the lines between memory and modernity. She embodies a woman in transition, grounded, luminous, and yet constantly becoming.
“I’m not very good at describing myself,” she says, almost in passing.
Yet, in the same breath, her choices reveal a quiet clarity of intent. “I don’t want to isolate my character. I need to understand the whole world it exists in.”
Between restraint and reinvention, stillness and movement, she exists in that rare in-between. Not fully defined, not confined. Just on the cusp of a wider sky.

posts.caption:
As booksellers try to figure out how to market audiobooks in person, Audible, the audiobook giant owned by Amazon, staged a pop-up bookstore — without any physical books.
The Audible Story House, a three-story emporium on the Lower East Side of Manhattan, has an event space for craft nights and author panels and a cafe selling matcha lattes and branded merch. The shop also has two large listening lounges equipped with surround-sound, listening booths where individuals or small groups can sample the audiobooks and a listening bar where patrons can consult a “story tender” to get personalized recommendations from a list of titles available on Audible.
“People want not only to have IRL experiences, they want things that are tangible and not just digital,” said James Finn, Audible’s global head of brand and content marketing.
Some independent booksellers have shifted their sales strategies in the hopes of winning over the audiobook consumer — and staying competitive against giants like Audible. Are bookless bookstores a solution? Tap the link in our bio to read the full story. Photos by @sethdcaplan

Stephen Colbert’s 11-season run behind the “Late Show” desk came to a close last night. And with that, there is one less man in a suit to watch on late-night television.
For the better part of a half-century, the suited late-night host has been one of TV’s stubborn constants. And Colbert hewed to the uniform: dark suit, pressed white shirt and unremarkable tie.
"His jackets were always single-breasted, and most of them were dark blue, with some gray peppered in. A recent charity auction revealed that his wardrobe included suits from Zegna and Giorgio Armani. Some were faintly striped or checked, but still, there was not much raffish about them," our fashion reporter @jacobwgallagher wrote.
A totem of conventionality, the suit has become a useful tool for many late-night hosts. But the look was not always so uniform. At the link in our bio, read more about how the suited late-night host became convention — and how Colbert dressed the part. Photo by Scott Kowalchyk/CBS

posts.caption:
Shein, the online retailer that has become the de facto face of ultra fast fashion, has acquired Everlane, a U.S. retailer branded as sustainable, according to Everlane’s chief executive.
In a deal that was finalized on Friday, L Catterton, the private equity firm backed by luxury conglomerate LVMH, sold its majority stake in Everlane. The company would not say how much it was sold for, but Puck News had reported over the weekend that the price was $100 million. The deal set off a wave of backlash for Everlane, whose consumers saw the proposed sale to the ultra fast fashion giant as a betrayal of the brand’s climate-conscious ethos.
Alfred Chang, Everlane’s chief executive, said in a statement to The New York Times that Everlane will “remain an independent brand” and keep its “sustainability commitments.” He framed the acquisition as a way to expand the brand’s global reach and “accelerate” their vision. Chang will remain in his role, according to a memo sent to staff by Chang and Everlane’s leadership and seen by The Times.
Read more details about the acquisition at the link in our bio. Photo by @justinkaneps

On Wednesday, Louis Vuitton took over the Frick museum in New York to unveil a cruise collection that was nominally a meditation on the uptown-downtown divide and the way fashion travels between the two.
"Imagine a smashup of the Belle Époque glamour of a private mansion turned museum, filled with soft-focus oils of society doyennes past, and Keith Haring graffiti art with its democratization of the exquisite, and you’ll get the idea," Vanessa Friedman, chief fashion critic for The New York Times wrote in her review of the show.
On the runway, there was kitschy Americana (denim, cowskin and motorcycle gangs), faded denim and a bedazzled Chinese food takeout container, resulting in a chaotic mix of clothes and cultures, much like New York itself. Read @vvfriedman's full review of the @louisvuitton show and see more photos at the link in our bio. Photos by Gray Adam/ABACA, via Shutterstock, Arturo Holmes/Getty Images

posts.caption:
Frida Kahlo didn’t become one of the most famous figures of the 20th century by chance. The popular image of the artist that persists in Barbie dolls and on coffee mugs was a carefully constructed avatar — proof, according to the costume designer Jon Bausor, that “she was a living work of art.”
Kahlo had an awareness of the power of visuals from an early age: She chose clothing that concealed her disability but highlighted her heritage; she darkened her eyebrows, but refused to separate them. And whether posing for a photo or sitting for one of her many three-quarter view self-portraits, she was always aware of her angles. Kahlo’s personal presentation was almost an extension of her artistic practice.
A new exhibition at the Museum of Modern Art features works by Kahlo and her husband, Diego Rivera. But to better understand Kahlo as a painter, it helps to understand her as a performance artist, forever putting on a show for the world. From her plaster corsets to her spider monkeys, learn about all the things that made Frida Frida at the link in our bio. Photos by @rickyrhodes, Sylvia Salmi/Bettmann, via Getty Images, Bettmann/Getty Images, Banco de México Diego Rivera Frida Kahlo Museums Trust, Mexico, CDMX / Artists Rights Society (ARS), New York, @laurenfleishman, @interiorphotography

His latest #Dior collection (first teased in November 25) features #TheVeryHungryCaterpillar by Eric Carle. It’s one of the most universally recognisable children’s books, instantly familiar across generations. For many including myself, it was one of the first books I encountered as a child. It’s fun to see Jonathan Anderson continue to mix Dior’s couture sensibilities with playful and whimsical references. The result feels nostalgic, ironic, emotional, and unserious, elevating childhood memory into collectible fashion.

posts.caption:
Many studies have shown that women are significantly less likely to receive bystander CPR in public compared to men. Despite many technological advancements in medicine, CPR training continues to rely on male manikins, creating a gap between training and real-world situations.
While the method of CPR remains the same regardless of gender, research links this bias to fear of inappropriate physical contact and uncertainty regarding chest compressions on female anatomy.
Female CPR manikins can be an important step toward addressing this disparity. By incorporating anatomically accurate female features into training simulations, these manikins can help rescuers become more confident and prepared to respond without hesitation.

Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.

posts.caption:
To the fashion stylist who is thinking about quitting.
This one is for you.
Thinking about leaving does not mean you are not good at this. Some of the best stylists I know have sat exactly where you are sitting right now. The quiet months, the clients who treat the work like a favour, the rate conversations you dread. It is a hard career, and you are not weak for finding it hard.
But before you make any decision, ask yourself one honest question. Have you fallen out of love with the work itself? Or are you just worn out by the way you have had to do it?
Because they are not the same thing. The stylists who walk away rarely go because the love died. They go because they were never saying no. No boundaries. Burnt out. Doing every job, for every client, at every rate. And the industry keeps the business side a secret, so you believe the burnout is just part of it.
It is not. The conditions are not fixed.
✦ The version of this career that wore you down is not the only one on offer. ✦
Your rate can change. Your clients can change. The way you run every part of this can change. So before you call it, give yourself a real shot at doing it differently.
💚 For every fashion stylist who has thought about walking away
💚 For every stylist who is burnt out and out of boundaries
💚 For every working fashion stylist who deserves to see what they are really capable of

There is what people think a fashion stylist does. And there is what we actually do.
They think we shop all day. We source against a brief, a budget, and a deadline, and buying is one minute of a fifty-hour week. They think we keep the clothes. We return every piece, on time, with paperwork, and protect every press relationship we have built. They think it is glamorous. We carry the bags in the rain and answer emails at eleven at night.
The glamour is the final image. The work is everything underneath it.
Anyone who has spent a single day on set knows the difference. The ones who keep getting it wrong are the ones who have never seen the work up close.

posts.caption:
A fashion stylist leaves school with one question. Now what?
The degree taught you the theory. The history. The construction. The language. What it did not teach you was how to find clients, set rates, build contacts, write a contract, manage returns, or recover from a job that went wrong. That curriculum starts on the first day of the working career and nobody hands you a syllabus.
This is a hands-on job, and the real learning happens on set. Assisting is where you earn it. Watching how a working stylist reads a room, builds a rail, handles a client, saves a shoot at four o’clock. The years spent assisting are not a detour before the career. They are the career, learning itself.
The portfolio you leave with is not the portfolio that gets you booked. The business side is on you to figure out, the same way every working stylist before you did, by getting it wrong first.
✦ You do not leave school a stylist. You become one with experience
The version of you five years into the job is doing work the student version could not have imagined. That is the only reassurance the working career will give you.
💚 For every fashion stylist who has just left school and is asking now what
💚 For every stylist three years in still figuring out the business side
💚 For every working fashion stylist who is the proof the course was the start, not the career

posts.caption: In most animal species, nature tends to maintain a roughly equal number of males and females because this balance maximizes reproductive success over generations. However, in many situations, populations may become female-biased because females are more important for increasing population size; a single male can reproduce with multiple females, but each female can produce only a limited number of offspring. In addition, males in many species face higher mortality due to fighting, competition, risk-taking, and greater visibility to predators. Some animals, such as bees and ants, naturally produce overwhelmingly female populations because only a few males are needed for reproduction. Thus, evolution does not intentionally “favor” females, but biological and environmental pressures often make female-heavy populations more advantageous for survival and recovery.

posts.caption:
There is a point where jewellery stops behaving like ornament and starts asking to be read. New York-based label Eskae (@eskae.atelier) sits in that space, creating sculptural pieces that challenge the polite expectations of modern jewellery. “I was inspired to start ESKAE by a desire to create jewellery that holds meaning beyond adornment… ESKAE is a play on the suffix ‘esque’, suggesting the essence of something rather than a fixed definition. I wanted to build a brand that feels like a state of being rather than a label, something that invites interpretation,” says founder Drishti Verma (@drishtivermaa).
Born in India and now based in New York City, Verma works between two cultural registers. India’s layered traditions and New York’s global landscape have shaped her understanding of “form, meaning and universality and how intimate belief systems can be shared and understood across different contexts.” In her work, that duality appears through contrast: structure and fluidity, restraint and expression, silence and intensity.
Drawing from mythology, philosophy and psychology, Eskae is anchored in what Verma calls the “unspoken”: emotional states that exist before language. The result is jewellery that feels sculptural, intimate and alive.

posts.caption:
In India, guilt has rarely been just a feeling. It sits inside silence, duty, family expectation, internalised shame and the promise of karmic correction.
Now it has another life online. A post, a reshare, a hashtag or the right therapy-speak can start to look like social accountability. Sometimes it is care. Sometimes it is performance. Often, it is both.
Privilege still decides who gets to look away. Women and marginalised groups are often left carrying the heaviest moral burden, while Gen Z is learning to draw boundaries and inherit a new guilt through visibility, outrage and the pressure to post.
What guilt reveals is less about goodness and more about power.
Read more at the link in bio.
By Karishma Kuenzang (@kkuenzang)
#GuiltInIndia #InternalisedGuilt #SocialAccountability #FamilyBoundaries #performativeactivism

This human artist creates oil paintings that literally look like dreams and I need one in my life immediately—and no, they’re not AI.
@diango.hernandez is a Cuban artist and designer whose work constantly gets mistaken for digital art online because the paintings are that surreal, but it’s all done by hand. Hernández blends his background in industrial design with traditional oil painting techniques, creating these hyper-precise compositions that still somehow feel emotional and unstable at the same time.
Honestly one of those artists that makes you stare at a painting for ten minutes trying to figure out how anyone could think human art could ever be replaced.
Literally insane.

posts.caption:
If any other fashion people out there are starting to fall for the art world, just know you’re not alone. Fashion and fine art naturally pull toward each other because they’re both visual languages rooted in emotion, identity, and cultural commentary. And honestly, after enough trend cycles and market reports, it makes sense that so many people in fashion start craving something a little slower, more permanent, and less commercially reactive.
A lot of the industry’s most interesting creatives eventually drift toward art because it offers a different kind of freedom — one less tied to seasons, algorithms, and wearability. The overlap has always existed, but right now it feels especially strong.
Maybe we’re all just looking for substance again (I’m projecting).
#Fashion #ArtWorld #ContemporaryArt #FashionCulture #TheCitizensPoste

posts.caption:
Look at anyone’s online bio today, and you’ll find a grocery list of skills and accomplishments. No one’s just one thing.
On ‘The Nod’ team masthead itself you’ll see an audience growth manager who runs a bookstagram. Our social media editor at work goes home to make comedic reels. And our fashion writer, we’re convinced, should have her own YouTube channel. It’s as 2026 as it can be.
Of course, our cover girl Samantha Ruth Prabhu (@samantharuthprabhuoffl) represents everything we’re talking about—movie star, producer, serial entrepreneur, chatty podcaster. A multi-hyphenate in the true sense of the term.
As The Nod turns two, all this month we will feature an impressive array of all-rounders: a brand strategist with an alter ego, a BAFTA-nominated actor dabbling in prog rock, a hustler with more jobs than days in a week, an architect who’s a drag queen…
But are these side hustles a great work-life blend to save us all from burnout or just faux-inspirational tales designed as coping mechanism? Is it a way to ensure that we’re relevant in a chaotic time where job security is dismal, technology is throwing new realities our way, and the world is at war? Or is it our unsatiable curiosity that is leading us there? This May, join us as we explore ‘The Age of the Poly-Professional’.

Finally, we're paying heed. Welcome to the age of universal polymathy, or, as we're calling it, the Age of the Poly-Professional.'.

posts.caption:
For Adarsh Gourav (@gouravadarsh)—the BAFTA-nominated actor who had the dream debut of playing a younger Shah Rukh Khan in ‘My Name is Khan’ (2010) and has, in the last 15 years, worked with bucket-list filmmakers like Zoya Akhtar, Bejoy Nambiar, Ramin Bahrani, and Arjun Varain Singh—music has always been his first love. It’s a love seeded in his childhood, one that has stuck despite often being unrequited, through rejection, failure, and harsh realisations. It has been his Everest, and it has given him everything he has today; he would likely not be on screen if it weren’t for being “noticed” while singing ‘Vande Mataram’ at the Kala Ghoda Arts Festival as a teenager, which really put him on the path to auditioning for films.
“I’ve always wanted to make music. I wanted to be a playback singer,” he says now, relaying a parallel life story that’s stayed under wraps until very recently. “And now I’m just using my street cred as an actor to push this side of myself. And why not? I hope they’ll think: ‘Acting achhi kar leta hai toh ye bhi theek thaak kar hi leta hoga.’”
At the link in bio, @_niddee_ chats with the polymath about the rare vantage of enjoying the best of both worlds.

Growing up in Chennai as the youngest of three siblings, acting wasn’t part of the plan for model student Samantha Ruth Prabhu (@samantharuthprabhuoffl). During her gap year after college, the then 21-year-old dabbled in modelling and ended up at a film audition for kicks. “I thought if I do get it, I’ll do it for a year and then go back to studying,” she says. “The idea was to do it temporarily, never make it a career. I’m sure my teachers were disappointed when I became an actor. I think some of them still are,” she laughs.
We’re not the first to ask her what her alternative career would be, but this time she has an answer: “I’m doing it now,” she says with excitement. “I think this entrepreneurial journey is what I’ve always wanted to do,” says the serial entrepreneur, whose work straddles films, beauty, wellness, fashion, food, and sport, all at once.

posts.caption:
At Goa’s Bambolim beach, a few kilometres from Panjim, the same scene plays out every morning. This is a quiet, shack-free crescent, with a thin strip of wet sand that rarely attracts any tourists. You’ll spot a few people out on their morning walk, a few out for their morning smoke, and a few friendly dogs stretching. And you’ll always—always—spot a large group of open-water swimmers. From the very young to the very young at heart, this group features an eclectic collection of professionals: cops, surgeons, dentists, teachers, techies, marketers, restaurateurs, all being trained by a man who’s got two very interesting careers of his own.
Indrajeet Khadilkar(@serious_about_training)is a Goa-based swimming coach who trains people of all ages and skill levels to swim long distances in the sea. When he’s not in the water, he’s helping cops and lawyers untangle complex legal and criminal matters, armed with his international forensics diploma and decades of experience as a handwriting analyst. How does one end up with such an unusual career combination? A former president probably had something to do with it.
At the link in bio, Deepak Narayanan chats with  Khadilkar about the two very different worlds he inhabits.

posts.caption:
“Cannes can start now!” declared actor Eva Longoria as she joined fellow L’Oréal Paris ambassador Aishwarya Rai Bachchan on stage at the cosmetic giant’s photo-op at the 79th Cannes Film Festival on Friday. And for many die-hard red carpet watchers, Cannes truly did begin this weekend as India’s uncrowned ‘Queen of Cannes’ stepped onto the Croisette once again. Rai Bachchan, arguably one of the festival’s most watched, debated and dissected celebrities, had already sent expectations soaring after news broke that stylist and designer Mohit Rai would helm her wardrobe this year.
For her first appearance, ARB arrived in an ‘abyss blue’ custom sculptural couture gown by Amit Aggarwal featuring his signature crystal vein embroidery. Day two saw her in a draped bodice gown by Sophie Couture complete with a cape-like dupatta detail. Were the outfits groundbreaking couture? No. The silhouettes, styling and dupatta flourishes weren’t too far removed from her usual wheelhouse. Did she look lovely? Absolutely. The colours worked beautifully on her and the beauty choices were refreshingly restrained (goodbye, purple lipstick). Were these looks for the books? Perhaps not.
But for the Bollywood star who has walked the Cannes red carpet since 2002—with a foot injury, through relentless body scrutiny post-pregnancy, with a broken arm, and through multiple other personal and professional battles we may not be privy to—there is still no writing her off. Now arriving with daughter Aaradhya Bachchan in tow, dressed in Gauri & Nainika no less, ARB remains far more than just another red carpet moment.
#Cannes #ARB #AishwaryaRaiBachchan

posts.caption:
The Cannes Film Festival, the world’s largest cinematic showcase, has become a litmus test for the industry’s evolving relationship with AI.  The 79th edition in France saw the event enter a multi-year partnership with Meta AI, replacing TikTok. It is a move consistent with tech entrepreneur Mark Zuckerberg’s attempt to move into soft power, and comes right after he was spotted at the Met Gala sponsored by fellow tech billionaire Jeff Bezos and wife Lauren Sanchez. Meta’s role is to ensure frenzied coverage of the festival, and respond to filmmakers’ openness to using AI tools. With the Ray-Ban meta glasses content creators are capturing photos and videos and livestreaming in real time. The aim—to make the festival accessible to audiences across the world. Cannes’ embrace of Meta feels jarring, especially in the face of The Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences’ announcement that only acting and writing for films done by humans will be considered eligible to win an Oscar. This declaration came after Dutch actor-producer Eline Van der Velden unveiled Tilly Norwood, the “world’s first AI actress” at Zurich Film Festival in September last year.
At Cannes, there is a sense of resignation and curiosity. Steven Soderbergh’s documentary on John Lennon and Yoko Ono, called John Lennon: The Last Interview, has used Meta’s AI tools to generate surreal graphics. Directors like Xavier Gens and Peter Jackson have expressed no qualms about working with the technology, but in the same breath emphasised the need to protect creatives. It is a dull, diplomatic tirade that skirts around the seismic shift this is likely to bring to filmmaking. Actor and jury member Demi Moore stated that the battle against AI is one humans are likely to lose—one of the more honest, albeit cynical, responses among the cohort. Director James Gray, whose Paper Tiger has emerged as a festival favourite, had something humanistic to offer: “I don’t think in our lifetime, or even our children’s lifetimes, it will come close to mirroring the only true infinite we know, which is the soul.” Whether Cannes continues to be soulmates with AI or not, only time (and Meta) will tell.
🖋️ @shrutijn6891
